title: <div>Every scene, every chapter, every moment in a story should have a pulse—a s
content:
Every scene, every chapter, every moment in a story should have a pulse—a shift in emotional energy.
• Scenes aren’t just things happening; they shift in meaning.
• A scene starts in one state and ends in another. (Positive → Negative, Negative → Positive)

Scene Start Action Scene End
Hopeful Protagonist receives bad news Desperate
Angry Argument turns into reconciliation Relieved
Confused Discovers a clue Determined

Activity: Scene Pulse Mapping
1. Write a short scene.
2. Identify the emotional state at the start.
3. Identify how the scene shifts by the end.
4. If there’s no shift, revise it to introduce a positive or negative pulse change.

3. Macro & Micro Dramatic Structure (Story as a Graph)

A story is like a wave, rising and falling with tension and resolution.
• Big Picture (Macro Pulse): The whole story has highs and lows.
• Small Picture (Micro Pulse): Each scene also has highs and lows.
context: From document: Writing Guide for Derek: Structure, Exploration, and Momentum — chunk 3 (chunk 3/8)
